section.title: The Fountain of Cleansing and the Struck Shepherd
section.sectionRationale: The mourning of chapter 12 flows straight into cleansing. On that day a fountain is opened for sin and impurity, idols and false prophets are swept away, and then the sword awakes against the LORD's own Shepherd: strike the Shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered. Jesus quoted this verse over His disciples on the night He was betrayed. The struck Shepherd and the opened fountain are two halves of one cross.
section.thesis: The fountain that cleanses sin is opened by the sword that strikes the Shepherd; the LORD wounds His own Companion so that a scattered, refined remnant may be washed, own His name, and be called His people.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the fountain opened for sin and uncleanness; the end of idols and false prophets, down to the prophet who denies his wounds; and the sword against the Shepherd, the sheep scattered, and a refined third brought through the fire to call on the LORD.

verses.text: “On that day a {{maqor|fountain}} will be opened to the house of David and the people of Jerusalem, to cleanse them from sin and {{niddah|impurity}}.

units.label: The end of idols and false prophets

verses.text: And on that day, declares the LORD of Hosts, I will erase the names of the idols from the land, and they will no longer be remembered. I will also remove the prophets and the spirit of impurity from the land.

And if anyone still prophesies, his father and mother who bore him will say to him, ‘You shall not remain alive, because you have spoken falsely in the name of the LORD.’ When he prophesies, his father and mother who bore him will pierce him through.
And on that day every prophet who prophesies will be ashamed of his vision, and he will not put on a hairy cloak in order to deceive.
He will say, ‘I am not a prophet; I work the land, for I was purchased as a servant in my youth.’
If someone asks him, ‘What are these wounds on your chest?’ he will answer, ‘These are the wounds I received in the house of my friends.’

units.label: The Shepherd struck, the sheep scattered
units.note: a third refined through the fire

verses.text: Awake, O sword, against My Shepherd, against the man who is My {{amit|Companion}}, declares the LORD of Hosts. Strike the Shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered, and I will turn My hand against the little ones.
And in all the land, declares the LORD, two-thirds will be cut off and perish, but a third will be left in it.

This third I will bring through the fire; I will {{tsaraph|refine}} them like silver and test them like gold. They will call on My name, and I will answer them. I will say, ‘They are My people,’ and they will say, ‘The LORD is our God.’”

maqor.gloss: A spring or fountain, the source from which water flows. Not a basin to be filled and emptied but a living, self-renewing source, opened for the house of David to wash away sin. The image answers the mourning of chapter 12: the very people who pierced the Lord are given a perpetual fountain of cleansing, opened, the New Testament shows, by His pierced side.

niddah.gloss: Impurity, defilement, the ritual uncleanness that barred a person from God's presence. Its use here says that sin is not merely guilt to be pardoned but filth to be washed; the fountain deals with both, cleansing from sin and from impurity, restoring the defiled to fellowship with a holy God.

amit.gloss: An associate, a near one, a fellow or companion who stands as an equal. Outside this verse the word appears almost entirely in Leviticus for one's neighbor and equal. For the LORD of Hosts to call the Shepherd the man who is My Companion is to set Him beside God as His peer, a staggering hint of the Shepherd's deity, fulfilled in the Son who is one with the Father.

tsaraph.gloss: To smelt, refine, or purify metal by fire, burning away the dross. The surviving third is not spared the fire but brought through it, refined like silver and tested like gold. Suffering here is not abandonment but the purifying hand of God, producing a people who call on His name and are answered as His own.

basic.context: The mourning of chapter 12 pours straight into cleansing. On that day God opens a fountain for the house of David to wash away sin and uncleanness. Then He purges the land of idols and false prophets, so thoroughly that a former prophet denies he ever was one and explains away the ritual gashes on his body. At the climax God calls a sword to awake against His own Shepherd, the man who is His Companion: strike the Shepherd, and the sheep scatter. Two-thirds perish, but a third is brought through the fire, refined, and restored as God's own people.
basic.hermeneutics: This is day of the LORD prophecy, tightly bound to chapter 12 and continued in chapter 14. The fountain and the struck Shepherd are read by the New Testament as fulfilled in Christ; Jesus Himself quotes verse 7 the night He is arrested. The main debate is the identity of the struck Shepherd, and whether verse 6 speaks of the false prophet's shame or, in an older reading, the wounds of Christ.
basic.originalLanguages: A maqor, a living fountain, is opened to cleanse from sin and from niddah, ritual impurity, so both guilt and defilement are washed away. The struck one is called the LORD's amit, His Companion or equal, a word used elsewhere almost only in Leviticus for a neighbor, hinting at the Shepherd's deity. And the surviving third is put through the fire and tsaraph, refined like silver, purified rather than destroyed.
basic.typology: The opened fountain foreshadows the cleansing flood from Christ's pierced side. The struck Shepherd whose sheep scatter is fulfilled in Gethsemane, quoted by Jesus over His fleeing disciples. The refined third that passes through fire and calls on God's name pictures the purified remnant, the church tested and owned as God's people.
basic.christ: Jesus applies verse 7 to Himself on the night of His betrayal: I will strike the Shepherd, and the sheep of the flock will be scattered, Matthew 26:31. The Shepherd called the LORD's Companion is the Son who is one with the Father, and the sword that awakes against Him is the just judgment God lays on Him in our place. The fountain of verse 1 is opened by that very stroke, so the struck Shepherd and the cleansing fountain are two sides of the one cross.
basic.god: God is holy: He purges idols, false prophets, and impurity, and He will not leave sin unaddressed. Yet He is astonishingly gracious: He opens a perpetual fountain for the guilty and refines a remnant through fire rather than destroying it. Most stunning of all, the sword falls by God's own command on His own Companion, so that the judgment sinners deserve lands on the Shepherd, and the scattered flock is gathered, cleansed, and owned as His people.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 26:31 quotes the struck Shepherd over the scattered disciples. John 19:34 shows blood and water from Jesus' pierced side, the fountain opened. 1 Peter 1:7 speaks of faith refined by fire like gold. Romans 9 to 11 traces the surviving, called remnant.
basic.oneStory: The mourning over the pierced One gives way to an opened fountain, and the fountain is opened by a sword that strikes God's own Shepherd. The flock scatters, but a refined remnant is brought through the fire to call on the LORD and be called His people. It all converges at Calvary, where the struck Shepherd bleeds the cleansing stream and gathers His scattered sheep.

context.literary: The middle panel of the second burden, joined seamlessly to chapter 12 by on that day and leading into chapter 14. The mourning over the pierced One (12:10) opens the fountain (13:1); the removal of false prophets (13:2-6) clears the land; and the struck Shepherd (13:7-9) explains how the fountain is opened and how the remnant is formed.
context.historical: To a restored community still tempted by idolatry and plagued by false prophecy, the promise of a purged land and an opened fountain spoke of a cleansing deeper than any reform they could manage. The struck Shepherd and the scattered flock pointed beyond their own leaders to a decisive act of God in the future.
context.canonical: Zechariah 13 stands with Isaiah 53 among the clearest Old Testament previews of a smitten Savior. Verse 1 is a fountainhead image taken up across Scripture for the cleansing power of Christ's blood, and verse 7 is quoted directly by Jesus, tying this chapter firmly to the passion.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the cleansing of God's people, the purging of false worship, and the forming of a faithful remnant all flow from a single act of God: the striking of His own Shepherd, by which a fountain for sin is opened.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter foretells what God will do; it is not a program for zealous relatives to execute or a call to seek suffering. Its enduring truths are the sufficiency of the opened fountain, the deity and substitution of the struck Shepherd, and the refining purpose of God in the trials of His people.

debates.question: Who is the Shepherd struck in verse 7?

views.label: The Messiah, Christ Himself
views.case: Jesus quotes the verse of His own arrest in Matthew 26:31, and the title My Companion sets the Shepherd beside God as His equal; the sword falls on Him by God's command, the substitution at the heart of the cross.

views.label: A representative shepherd figure under God's judgment
views.case: Some read the struck Shepherd more broadly within the shepherd theme of chapters 11 to 13; yet even on this reading the New Testament directs the verse decisively to Christ struck for the sheep.

debates.bearing: Both are governed by Jesus' own use of the verse: the struck Shepherd is Christ, and His striking scatters the sheep yet gathers a remnant.

debates.question: What are the wounds in verse 6, on the chest or between the hands?

views.label: The false prophet's shameful marks
views.case: In context the verse continues the exposure of the false prophet, whose ritual self-gashing or beating in an idolatrous house he now tries to explain away; the wounds are evidence of his discredited trade.

views.label: An older messianic reading of Christ's wounds
views.case: A long devotional tradition heard here the wounds Christ received in the house of His friends, the marks in His hands; most today place this verse with the false prophet, while granting its language has drawn hearts to the wounded Savior.

debates.bearing: The chapter's clear messianic weight rests on verses 1 and 7; verse 6 is best read of the exposed false prophet, without denying how its words have pointed many to the wounds of Christ.

study.originalLanguages: Three words carry the chapter. First, a maqor is opened, a living fountain rather than a basin, an ever-flowing spring to cleanse the house of David from sin and from niddah, ritual impurity, so that both the guilt and the defilement of sin are washed away at their source. Second, the struck one is named the LORD's amit, His Companion, a rare word found almost only in Leviticus for one's neighbor and equal; for the LORD of Hosts to call the Shepherd My Companion is to place Him at God's own side as His peer, a quiet but breathtaking claim to the Shepherd's deity, fulfilled in the Son who is one with the Father. Third, the surviving third is not spared the furnace but brought through it and tsaraph, refined like silver, smelted so the dross burns away, until a purified people calls on the LORD's name. Fountain, Companion, and refining fire together tell how the cross cleanses, who the Shepherd is, and what God makes of those He saves.

typology.type: The fountain opened for sin and impurity
typology.fulfillment: The cleansing stream from Christ's pierced side, His blood that washes away sin, John 19:34 and 1 John 1:7.

typology.type: The Shepherd struck and the sheep scattered
typology.fulfillment: Jesus struck at His arrest, His disciples scattered, quoted by Him in Matthew 26:31.

typology.type: The third refined through fire and called God's people
typology.fulfillment: The remnant purified by trial, calling on the Lord and owned as His people, 1 Peter 1:7 and Romans 9:25-27.

study.christ: This chapter is one of the great passion prophecies of the Old Testament, and Jesus Himself unlocks it. On the night of His betrayal He tells the eleven, You will all fall away on account of Me this night, for it is written, I will strike the Shepherd, and the sheep of the flock will be scattered, Matthew 26:31. The struck Shepherd is Christ; the scattered sheep are His disciples fleeing in Gethsemane. And the astonishment of the verse is who wields the sword: the LORD of Hosts commands it to awake against His own Shepherd, the man who is My Companion, My equal. Here is the cross laid bare: the judgment we deserved falls, by the Father's own hand, on the Son who is one with Him. And this striking is no tragedy that thwarts God's purpose; it is the very means by which the fountain of verse 1 is opened, for it is from the pierced side of the struck Shepherd that blood and water flow to cleanse the house of David. So the two halves of the chapter are two sides of one saving act: the Shepherd is struck, and the fountain gushes; the sheep scatter, and a refined remnant is gathered to call on His name. Every believer who has been washed drinks from a fountain opened by the sword that fell on Jesus.
study.god: The God of this chapter is fiercely holy and tenderly saving at once. He will not tolerate idols or lying prophets; He erases their names and purges the spirit of impurity from the land, and He refines His remnant in a furnace hot enough to burn away every impurity. Yet the same God opens a perpetual fountain for sinners who have no power to cleanse themselves, and He passes His people through the fire not to consume them but to purify them, so that on the far side they call on His name and He answers, they say the LORD is our God and He says they are My people. The deepest revelation is that the sword falls by His own command on His own Companion. God does not spare His Shepherd; He strikes Him, so that the guilty flock might be washed. Here justice and mercy meet: the judgment is real and it is paid in full, but it is paid by God Himself in the person of the smitten Shepherd, so that cleansing flows freely to all who mourn and look and call.

crossReferences.note: Jesus quotes strike the Shepherd and the sheep will be scattered of His own arrest and the disciples' flight.
Blood and water flow from Jesus' pierced side, the opened fountain of cleansing.
Faith refined by fire, more precious than gold, matching the tested and refined third.
A called people who were not My people, and a saved remnant, echoing verse 9.

study.oneStory: The mourning over the pierced One opens a fountain for sin, and the fountain is opened by a sword that strikes God's own Shepherd. The flock scatters, two-thirds fall, but a refined third is carried through the fire to call on the LORD and be owned as His people. It all converges at Calvary, where the struck Shepherd bleeds the cleansing stream, gathers His scattered sheep, and makes of them a purified people who say, the LORD is our God.

sermon.title: There Shall Be a Fountain Opened
sermon.why: Piper preaches Zechariah 13:1 as the most important promise in the book, arguing that the old sacrifices could not take away sin and that the fountain still to be opened was the pierced side of the Son of God, into which he draws the mourning over the pierced One of 12:10; a careful, Christ-centered exposition of this passage.

quiz.q: What is opened on that day, and for what purpose?

options.text: A fountain, to cleanse the house of David from sin and impurity.
options.note: Verse 1: a fountain opened to wash away sin and uncleanness.

options.text: A treasury, to make the people wealthy.
options.note: The fountain is for cleansing from sin, not for riches.

options.text: A gate, to let the nations invade.
options.note: The image is a cleansing fountain, not an open gate.

options.text: Nothing of lasting significance.
options.note: The fountain is the foundation of every other promise in the book.

quiz.q: Who is the Shepherd struck by the sword in verse 7?

options.text: The LORD's own Companion, applied by Jesus to Himself at His arrest.
options.note: Jesus quotes this verse in Matthew 26:31 of His own striking.

options.text: A foreign general defeated in battle.
options.note: The struck one is the LORD's Companion, fulfilled in Christ.

options.text: One of the false prophets of verse 2.
options.note: The Shepherd is God's equal, not a false prophet.

options.text: No one; it is only poetic imagery.
options.note: Jesus treats it as a real prophecy fulfilled in Him.

quiz.q: What does the title My Companion reveal about the Shepherd?

options.text: He stands beside God as His equal, a hint of the Shepherd's deity.
options.note: Amit denotes a near equal, setting the Shepherd at God's own side.

options.text: He is merely a hired hand with no special standing.
options.note: The word marks Him as God's equal, not a hireling.

options.text: He is an enemy of God.
options.note: He is God's Companion, struck by God's own command for the sheep.

options.text: The title means nothing in particular.
options.note: It is a striking claim to the Shepherd's equality with God.

quiz.q: What happens to the surviving third of the people?

options.text: They are brought through the fire, refined, and owned as God's people.
options.note: Verse 9: refined like silver, calling on God, called His own.

options.text: They are spared all hardship and left untested.
options.note: They are refined through fire, not spared it.

options.text: They are cut off along with the rest.
options.note: The third is preserved and purified, not cut off.

options.text: They are abandoned by God forever.
options.note: God calls them His people and answers when they call.

quiz.q: How are the fountain and the struck Shepherd related?

options.text: The fountain of cleansing is opened by the sword that strikes the Shepherd, both fulfilled at the cross.
options.note: The struck Shepherd's pierced side pours out the cleansing stream.

options.text: They are unrelated events far apart in time.
options.note: The chapter binds them as two sides of one saving act.

options.text: The fountain washes away the need for any Shepherd.
options.note: The fountain flows precisely because the Shepherd is struck.

options.text: The Shepherd blocks the fountain from the people.
options.note: The Shepherd's striking is what opens the fountain.

questions: A fountain was opened for sin and impurity. Are you still trying to wash yourself, or have you come to the fountain God has opened in Christ?
God purged idols and false prophets from the land. What false voices or hidden idols is God calling you to let Him remove?
The sword fell on the Shepherd in your place. How does it change your view of the cross to see that God struck His own Companion for you?
The remnant was refined, not spared, by fire. Where is God using trial to purify rather than to punish you, and can you trust His hand in it?
The struck Shepherd gathers scattered sheep. Where have you scattered from Him, and how does His promise to gather you draw you back?

translationNotes.note: Fountain renders maqor, a living spring or source, not a mere pool; it is opened, an ongoing, sufficient supply of cleansing rather than a one-time washing.

Wounds on your chest translates a phrase meaning literally between your hands; in context it describes the exposed false prophet, though its wording has long been applied devotionally to the wounds of Christ.
My Companion renders amit, a word used almost only in Leviticus for a near equal or neighbor; here it marks the Shepherd as God's peer, and Jesus applies the verse to Himself in Matthew 26:31.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Zechariah 13:1-9. All 9 verses reproduced in order. Poetic line structure in verses 7 to 9 rendered as continuous prose per house style; no em or en dashes in the source verses; footnote markers omitted from the running text.
